original_abstract: This study examines the principle of popular sovereignty in the context of Indonesian local elections under the 1945 Constitution. A central legal debate persists regarding the interpretation of the phrase "democratically elected" in Article 18, Paragraph (4). Two primary schools of thought have emerged: the first advocates for direct popular elections, while the second argues that elections conducted via regional parliaments (DPRD) fulfill the constitutional mandate. By analyzing related constitutional provisions, this paper argues that local elections are distinct from general elections—such as those for the presidency and legislative bodies. Ultimately, the study posits that a representative election system managed by the DPRD is a valid democratic mechanism that reflects popular sovereignty through the lens of Pancasila values, aligning with the original vision of Indonesia’s founding fathers.
humanized_abstract: This paper analyzes about the principle of popular sovereignty in Indonesian local election according to the 1945 Constitution. Until now the constitution interpretation in article 18 paragraph (4) about the word “democratically elected” that has not been decided yet, there are many interpretations from several groups. Some of them argue that elections must be held directly by the people and another one argues that elections can be held by regional parliament through a voting process. Based on several constitutional sections about the electoral system in Indonesia, the electoral system in local election is not the same as with national electoral system, namely the presidential and legislative elections. Through the constitutional analysis in this paper, it is stated that the representative election system which is carried out by the regional parliament is a democratic electoral process for upholding the principle of popular sovereignty with Pancasila values as its reference, the same intention with Indonesia’s founding fathers.
